title: On the true low-energy excitations of the three-dimensional spin glass
authors: Claudio Chilin, Enzo Marinari, Víctor Martín-Mayor, Giorgio Parisi, Juan J. Ruiz-Lorenzo, David Yllanes
abstract: We study the low-energy excitations of the three dimensional spin glass through a large-scale Monte Carlo simulation on lattices up to $L=18$. We find smooth extrapolations down to zero temperature, which, in the case of the energy and of the link overlap, can be directly -- and favourably -- compared with previous investigations featuring ground states (i.e., at zero temperature). The best fit for the fractal dimension of the excitations is provided by Replica-Symmetry Breaking theory, but we also consider the alternative TNT description. The $P(q)$ is found to verify the Parisi-Toulouse temperature scaling. Our data provides a spectacular confirmation of the overlap-equivalence hypothesis.
summary: This work uses large-scale Monte Carlo simulations to investigate the low-energy excitations of 3D spin glasses. By extrapolating results down to zero temperature, the authors find that Replica-Symmetry Breaking theory provides the best fit for key scaling parameters. The findings offer strong numerical confirmation of the overlap-equivalence hypothesis in disordered magnetic systems.
main_problem: The study aims to determine which theoretical framework (RSB, DM, CP, or TNT) correctly describes the low-energy excitations of three-dimensional spin glasses.
main_result: The simulations provide strong evidence supporting the Replica-Symmetry Breaking theory for the fractal dimension and confirm the overlap-equivalence hypothesis through smooth extrapolations to zero temperature.
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations are performed on lattices up to L=18, involving complex extrapolation techniques to reach the thermodynamic limit (L->infinity) and zero temperature (T->0).
model_or_system: The system studied is a three-dimensional spin glass (Edwards-Anderson type), modeled by Ising spins ($\sigma_i = \pm 1$) interacting via random couplings ($J_{ij}$). The analysis focuses on the behavior of the spin overlap parameter $q$ and the link overlap $q_l$.
key_observables: Spin overlap ($q$), link overlap ($q_l$), energy density ($\langle H/V angle$), fractal dimension ($d_f$), and the probability distribution $P(q)$.
important_parameters: System size L (up to 18), Temperature T (extrapolated down to zero), Dimension D=3.
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include the commutativity of limits ($T 	o 0$ and $L 	o \infty$) and the overlap-equivalence hypothesis, which links link overlap to the spin overlap.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate Parisi-Toulouse scaling for $P(q)$ vs T, and show extrapolations of $E(q_l|q_2=0)$ versus system size L and temperature T using various fitting ansatze.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the physical problem (low-energy excitations), detailing observables ($q, q_l$), presenting simulation results showing smooth extrapolations to $T=0$, and finally comparing these findings against predictions from established theoretical frameworks like RSB theory.
why_interesting: While focused on classical statistical mechanics, the rigorous numerical techniques used for extrapolation and phase boundary determination are highly relevant to understanding complex energy landscapes in quantum many-body systems, particularly those exhibiting glassy behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Parisi-Toulouse scaling. Temperature and size dependence for the cumu- lative probability Prob[q2 < 0.01], see Eq. (16), which provides a numerically stable estimate of P(q = 0). Left: Prob[q2 < 0.01] versus temperature, as computed for all our fully equilibrated lattice sizes. Right: Closeup of the T ∈[0,0.65] region, where we show Prob[q2 < 0.01]/T; PaT scaling predicts that Prob[q2 < 0.01]/T should be temperature independent. The grey horizontal band corresponds to Prob[q2 < 0.01; L = 6, T = 0.05] plus or minus its error. Excepting L = 4, all our data is compatible with this band if T < 0.5. The key is common to the two panels.

Figure 2: Link overlap as a function of q2. Plot of E(ql|q2), see Eq. (7), as com- puted for the lowest temperatures and for the biggest sizes we have simulated. In both panels errors are computed using a jackknife method. Left: estimate of E(ql|q2) for L = 14,16,18 at T = 0.2. Right: estimate of E(ql|q2) for L = 16 at various temperatures. The inset shows a closeup of the highlighted box.

Figure 3: Extrapolating E(ql|q2) to q2 = 0. Through the procedure explained in Sec. 3.2 we get interpolated data points for equally spaced temperatures 0.2 ≤T < 0.3. Left: E(ql|q2) at T = 0.2 for different linear sizes L. Dots repre- sent the measurements, dashed lines are fits to a linear function, solid lines are fits to a quadratic function. In order to get cleaner results, fits have been restricted to the intervals shown in Appendix A.2. Right: E(ql|q2 = 0) for L = 16 at different temperatures T. Blue and orange dots are the values of the intercepts obtained from the fits. Green dots are the average between the two, as explained in the main text. The grey curve is the cubic-spline...

Figure 4: Taking the successive L →∞and T →0 limits. Left: E(ql|q2 = 0) for T = 0.2 as a function of 1/L. The dashed line is the fit of these points to Eq. (20). The resulting parameters, along with the goodness-of-fit metrics, are reported in Table 2. Right: L →∞values from fits of data at different temperatures to ansatz (20). The blue dot represents the result from the fit shown in the left panel, the orange dots are obtained by fitting the interpolated data to the same function but fixing parameter c to the value in Table 2. Green and red dashed lines are the linear and quadratic fits of the orange points, respectively.

Figure 5: Energy density as a function of T. Plot of our extrapolations for the thermodynamic limit of the energy density e∞(T) obtained at fixed T from the fit to Eq. (24). The grey dashed line is a fit to Eq. (27), the corresponding values and accuracy parameters are reported in Table 3.
